title: Short n' Sweet
ratingVerdict: A tight, confident pop record that knows exactly what it is and delivers. Nothing reinvented, everything executed.
label: Island Records
intro: I resisted this one longer than I should have. Dismissed it as the algorithm's pick of the season. Then 'Taste' came on at a coffee shop and I sat there replaying it four times before I ordered anything.
Short n' Sweet doesn't pretend to be anything other than what it is: twelve songs of extremely polished pop with better jokes than you're expecting. That turns out to be enough.
paragraphs: Carpenter's writing is sharper here than it gets credit for. The album runs on a very specific kind of wit — dry, a little mean, delivered with a smile. 'Please Please Please' is the obvious one, but 'Coincidence' does it better: a song about a situationship told entirely through things that just keep happening to happen. The restraint is the joke.

Production-wise it's almost aggressively clean — Amy Allen and Julian Bunetta keep everything at the same polished sheen. That's the one place the album loses me slightly. A rougher edge somewhere would have made the best songs hit harder. But there's also something to be said for a record that sounds this confident in its lane.
pullQuote: Twelve songs, zero filler, and more memorable one-liners than most albums twice its length.

standoutTracks.title: Taste
standoutTracks.review: The opener sets the tone immediately — playful, a little cutting, with a hook that sticks whether you want it to or not. Perfect 'I told you so' energy.

standoutTracks.title: Please Please Please
standoutTracks.review: The single everyone knows. It's good and she knows it's good and that confidence is kind of the whole point of the song.

standoutTracks.title: Coincidence
standoutTracks.review: The funniest song on the record, which also makes it the saddest. The whole premise is a lie the narrator is telling herself in real time. It sneaks up on you.

standoutTracks.title: Don't Smile
standoutTracks.review: The one moment where the production opens up a little. A slower pace suits her voice well and the bridge lands harder for it.
